questions.question: In the SQL Server security model, what is the correct division of responsibility between a **login** and a **database user**?
options: A login holds permissions inside one database; a database user authenticates the connection at the server level
A login authenticates a connection at the server level; a database user holds permissions inside one database
Both are server-level principals stored in `master`; the user is just an alias for the login
A login is a per-table grant, while a database user is a per-schema grant
questions.explanation: A login (server-level, in `master`) proves who you are; a database user (database-level, usually mapped by SID) decides what you may do in that one database. The other options invert or confuse the two scopes.

questions.question: Every authorization check in SQL Server evaluates which triad?
options: login → password → connection
schema → table → column
principal → securable → permission
role → user → login
questions.explanation: Authorization is nothing more than checking whether a principal (login, user, role, app role) has a permission (SELECT, EXECUTE, ...) on a securable (server, database, schema, table, column, procedure).

questions.question: What does `REVOKE` do, and how does it differ from `DENY`?
options: It removes a prior grant or deny, returning to neutral; the user may still get the permission through another role
It explicitly forbids the permission and overrides any grant, just like `DENY` but only at table scope
It is an alias for `DENY` and guarantees the user can never access the securable
It grants the permission to everyone except the named principal
questions.explanation: `REVOKE` simply deletes the grant/deny row (back to neutral); it is not a deny, so the user can still inherit the permission via another role. To guarantee no access you must use `DENY`.

questions.question: Dynamic data masking (DDM) is a true security boundary: once a column is masked, a user without `UNMASK` cannot recover the real values stored in that column.
questions.explanation: DDM only obfuscates values at display time. The real data stays in the table, indexes, and backups, so an unprivileged user can filter, cast, join, or copy the column to infer or extract the actual values.

questions.question: What threat does Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) primarily protect against?
options: A malicious DBA running normal SELECT queries against the live database
Snooping the network wire between the client and the server
An over-privileged application account running ad-hoc DML
Stolen media — a lifted backup or detached `.mdf`/`.ldf` data files at rest
questions.explanation: TDE encrypts data and log files (and backups) at rest, so a thief with the raw files gets ciphertext. It is transparent to any legitimate privileged query (the DBA still sees plaintext), does not protect the wire, and is not an authorization control.

questions.question: A user has `DENY SELECT ON dbo.Orders` and also `GRANT SELECT ON dbo.Orders(Amount)`. Ignoring `sysadmin`, what is the user's effective access to `dbo.Orders`?
options: They can read only the `Amount` column — the column-level GRANT overrides the object-level DENY
They can read nothing — the table-level DENY overrides every grant, including the column grant
They can read every column — the GRANT promotes them to full table access
The two statements conflict and SQL Server raises an error at grant time
questions.explanation: The documented exception to 'DENY wins' is that a column-level GRANT overrides an object-level (table) DENY. So `Amount` remains readable even with a table-level DENY SELECT (this quirk is suppressed only when Common Criteria compliance is enabled).

questions.question: A user has `EXECUTE` on `dbo.usp_GetData` but no `SELECT` on `dbo.Salaries`, which the proc reads. The proc and table share owner `dbo`. The query succeeds. Now the proc is rewritten to read the table via `EXEC(@sql)`. What happens?
options: It now fails — the ownership chain breaks inside dynamic SQL, so permissions are re-checked against the caller, who lacks SELECT
It still succeeds — dynamic SQL preserves the chain because the proc is still owned by `dbo`
It still succeeds — granting EXECUTE permanently gives the caller SELECT on every table the proc touches
It fails even in the original version — EXECUTE never lets a proc read a table the caller can't
questions.explanation: Ownership chaining skips the permission check on referenced objects only when they share the same owner AND access is static. Inside `EXEC(@sql)`/`sp_executesql` the chain breaks and permissions are re-checked against the caller, who lacks SELECT.

questions.question: What is the senior least-privilege pattern for an application's database account?
options: Membership in `db_datareader` plus `db_datawriter` so the ORM can read and write any table
Membership in `db_owner` so schema migrations and DML both work without extra grants
A custom role with `EXECUTE` on a curated procedure set and no direct table access, relying on ownership chaining
A login mapped to `sysadmin` but restricted to a single database via the default schema
questions.explanation: The least-privilege design gives the app a custom role with EXECUTE on a curated proc surface and no table grants; ownership chaining lets the procs touch the tables. A leaked connection string then can't run ad-hoc DML, DROP TABLE, or bulk-exfiltrate. db_owner/db_datawriter/sysadmin are the opposite of least privilege.

questions.question: After restoring a database to a brand-new instance, an application can no longer connect even though its user still exists in the database. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The user is orphaned — its SID no longer matches any server login; re-link it with `ALTER USER ... WITH LOGIN = ...`
The user's password expired — reset it with `ALTER USER ... WITH PASSWORD`
The database is read-only after restore — run `ALTER DATABASE ... SET READ_WRITE`
TDE was lost in the restore — recreate the Database Encryption Key to restore login access
questions.explanation: An orphaned user is a database user whose SID no longer maps to any server login, common after restoring to a new instance. Detect by comparing `sys.database_principals` to `sys.server_principals`; fix with `ALTER USER ... WITH LOGIN = ...`.

questions.question: Which statements about Row-Level Security FILTER and BLOCK predicates are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A FILTER predicate silently removes non-matching rows from reads (and read-modify writes) without raising an error
A BLOCK predicate raises an error on write operations that would violate the rule, e.g. inserting a row a tenant could not see
The predicate is an inline table-valued function bound to a table by a `SECURITY POLICY`
A FILTER predicate raises `permission denied` for every row the principal cannot see
BLOCK predicates apply to `SELECT` reads, hiding rows the same way FILTER does
questions.explanation: FILTER silently removes rows (no error); BLOCK raises an error on offending writes (AFTER INSERT/UPDATE, BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE). Both are inline TVFs attached via a SECURITY POLICY. FILTER never errors per row, and BLOCK governs writes, not SELECT reads.

questions.question: You need to JOIN and run equality lookups on an Always Encrypted column. Which encryption type must you choose, and what is the trade-off?
options: Deterministic — same plaintext yields same ciphertext, so equality/joins/indexing work, but it is more vulnerable to inference on low-cardinality data
Randomized — it is the only type that supports server-side equality, at the cost of larger ciphertext
Deterministic — it allows full range comparisons and `LIKE` without a secure enclave
Randomized — it supports joins because the ciphertext is salted identically across rows
questions.explanation: Deterministic encryption produces identical ciphertext for identical plaintext, enabling equality lookups, joins, grouping, and indexing, but is more vulnerable to inference on low-cardinality columns. Randomized is more secure but supports no server-side operations (range/LIKE need a 2019+ enclave).

questions.question: Granting `SELECT ON SCHEMA::reporting` to a role gives that role SELECT on every current object in the `reporting` schema as well as any objects added to it later.
questions.explanation: A schema is both a namespace and a unit for granting permissions. A schema-level grant cascades to all contained securables, including future objects, which is why grouping objects by access profile and granting at the schema level is cleaner than per-table grants.

questions.question: User `maria` is in `Sales_RW` (`GRANT SELECT ON dbo.Orders`) and `PII_Lockdown` (`DENY SELECT ON dbo.Orders(CreditCardNo)`). She is not a `sysadmin`. What happens when she runs `SELECT * FROM dbo.Orders;`?
options: The statement fails — `*` includes `CreditCardNo`, the column DENY overrides the table grant, so the query is rejected
It succeeds and `CreditCardNo` is returned as NULL for every row
It succeeds and `CreditCardNo` is silently dropped from the result set
It succeeds in full because the table-level GRANT covers all columns
questions.explanation: `SELECT *` touches `CreditCardNo`, which is denied; DENY wins over the table grant, so the whole statement is rejected (Msg 230, SELECT permission denied). She must explicitly omit the denied column. RLS-style silent removal does not apply to column DENY.

questions.question: Compliance requires that even a `sysadmin` DBA cannot read patient SSNs. Why does Always Encrypted satisfy this when TDE does not?
options: Always Encrypted encrypts in the client driver and the engine never holds the keys, so a DBA query returns only ciphertext; TDE decrypts pages in memory, so a live query returns plaintext
Always Encrypted stores the Column Master Key inside the database so only the application schema can decrypt it, blocking the DBA
Always Encrypted runs the decryption inside `tempdb`, which sysadmins cannot access
Both expose plaintext to a DBA; only a column-level DENY can hide it from a sysadmin
questions.explanation: Always Encrypted draws the trust boundary at the client driver: keys (CMK/CEK) live with the app, so the engine stores only ciphertext and cannot decrypt. TDE decrypts pages into memory on read, so any live privileged query sees plaintext. Note sysadmin bypasses permission checks, so a column DENY cannot stop it.

questions.question: A team binds an RLS FILTER predicate whose inline TVF does an expensive lookup with a non-indexed correlated subquery. Why is this a serious problem beyond the obvious cost?
options: The predicate becomes part of every query plan against the table and runs under the query's context, so it taxes every read on the table
RLS re-evaluates the predicate once per connection only, so the cost is amortized and harmless
A FILTER predicate runs with elevated DBO context, so an expensive lookup risks privilege escalation, not a slowdown
The predicate executes only on writes, so it slows INSERT/UPDATE but never SELECT
questions.explanation: The predicate is appended to the plan and runs under the query's permissions/context on every query against the table, so a heavy predicate taxes all reads. It must be cheap, ideally `SELECT 1 WHERE ...` against an indexed lookup. It is not amortized per connection, and FILTER applies to reads.

questions.question: Which statements about TDE's key hierarchy and operational consequences are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: The chain runs Service Master Key → Database Master Key → certificate → Database Encryption Key, which performs the AES encryption of pages
If you lose the certificate and its private key, the encrypted backups become unrecoverable
Once any database on the instance uses TDE, `tempdb` is also encrypted
TDE keeps pages encrypted in memory too, so a memory dump yields ciphertext
The Database Encryption Key is stored in the client's key store, never on the server
questions.explanation: The hierarchy is SMK → DMK → certificate (or asymmetric/EKM key) → DEK → AES page encryption; losing the cert+private key loses the backups; and tempdb is encrypted once any DB on the instance uses TDE. Pages are decrypted in memory (not ciphertext there), and the DEK lives on the server, not the client (that is Always Encrypted's CMK).

questions.question: Why does a contained database largely eliminate orphaned-user problems on restore/failover?
options: Its users authenticate against the database itself with `CREATE USER ... WITH PASSWORD`, so authentication metadata travels with the database and there is no server login SID to re-map
It automatically recreates matching server logins on the target instance during restore
It disables SID-based mapping and authorizes every user as `dbo` after a restore
It stores all logins in `master` on the target instance before the restore completes
questions.explanation: A contained database carries its own authentication metadata; users authenticate directly against the DB (no server login), so moving/failing over the database needs no login re-creation or SID re-mapping, the very thing that orphans users. It does not auto-create server logins or authorize everyone as dbo.

questions.question: In EF Core, which call is injection-unsafe when fed user input, and what is the safe replacement?
options: `a` is unsafe — `FromSqlRaw` with string interpolation concatenates the input as SQL; use `FromSqlInterpolated` (or `{0}` placeholders), which binds it as a parameter
`b` is unsafe — `FromSqlInterpolated` always concatenates the interpolated value verbatim into the SQL text
Both are unsafe because any raw SQL in EF Core bypasses parameterization entirely
Both are safe because EF Core escapes all interpolated strings before sending them to SQL Server
questions.explanation: `FromSqlRaw` with an interpolated string concatenates user input into the SQL text and is injectable. `FromSqlInterpolated` (and `{0}` placeholders) turns interpolated values into bound `SqlParameter`s, so they can never be parsed as SQL. EF Core does not auto-escape `FromSqlRaw`.
